name: Greenlee, Eric T.
recent_1: Hess, L. J., & Greenlee, E. T. (2024). Auditory vigilance task performance and cerebral hemodynamics: effects of spatial uncertainty. Experimental Brain Research, 242(7), 1787-1795. DOI: 10.1007/s00221-024-06857-0
recent_2: Greenlee, E. T., Hess, L. J., Simpson, B. D., & Finomore, V. S. (2024). Vigilance to Spatialized Auditory Displays: Initial Assessment of Performance and Workload. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 66(4), 987-1003. DOI: 10.1177/00187208221139744
recent_3: McGarry, A., Neilson, B., Brown, T., Strong, R., Greenlee, E. T., Klein, M. I., & Coyne, J. (2023). An investigation of cardiac vagal tone over time and its relation to vigilance performance: a growth curve modeling approach. Frontiers in Neuroergonomics, 4, 1244658. DOI: 10.3389/fnrgo.2023.1244658
recent_1_synopsis: This study examined how spatial uncertainty about where a target will appear affects auditory vigilance performance and cerebral blood-flow (hemodynamic) responses measured via transcranial Doppler. Correct detections declined over the vigil, extending the neuroergonomic account of vigilance to spatially uncertain auditory monitoring and informing the design of auditory alert displays.
recent_2_synopsis: This paper provided an initial assessment of performance and perceived workload when monitoring spatialized (3D) auditory displays during a vigilance task. It establishes that spatialized auditory vigilance is demanding and shows the characteristic decrement, informing the use of 3D audio in monitoring and alerting systems.
recent_3_synopsis: Using growth-curve modeling, this study tracked cardiac vagal tone over time and related its dynamics to vigilance performance. It contributes a physiological (autonomic) index of the temporal course of sustained attention, advancing the neuroergonomic toolkit for tracking and potentially predicting vigilance decrements.
seminal_1: Greenlee, E. T., DeLucia, P. R., & Newton, D. C. (2018). Driver Vigilance in Automated Vehicles: Hazard Detection Failures Are a Matter of Time. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 60(4), 465-476. DOI: 10.1177/0018720818761711
seminal_2: Greenlee, E. T., DeLucia, P. R., & Newton, D. C. (2018). Driver Vigilance in Automated Vehicles: Effects of Demands on Hazard Detection Performance. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 61(3), 474-487. DOI: 10.1177/0018720818802095
seminal_1_synopsis: This study demonstrated that drivers monitoring an automated vehicle suffer a vigilance decrement, with hazard-detection failures increasing as a function of time on task. With roughly 143 citations (Human Factors metadata / Google Scholar, June 2026), it is Greenlee's most-cited work and a foundational reference establishing that prolonged passive monitoring of automation degrades drivers' ability to detect rare hazards.
seminal_2_synopsis: A follow-up study showing that increased task demands during automated-vehicle monitoring further impair hazard detection, clarifying the conditions under which the driver vigilance decrement is most severe. With roughly 39 citations (Human Factors metadata / Google Scholar, June 2026), it is a foundational companion piece deepening understanding of how workload and demand shape supervisory monitoring failures in automated driving.
expertise_overview: Greenlee studies vigilance and sustained attention-particularly the vigilance decrement, the temporal decline in detecting rare, critical signals during prolonged monitoring. A central thread is the neuroergonomics of vigilance, using physiological measures such as transcranial Doppler sonography of cerebral blood-flow velocity, cardiac/autonomic indices, and workload and stress assessment to test resource-depletion accounts of attention. He examines vigilance across sensory modalities (visual, auditory/spatialized auditory, and tactile) and probes supramodal versus modality-specific resource depletion. A major application domain is driver vigilance in automated vehicles, where he has shown that monitoring the roadway for hazards during automation produces a time-based decrement in hazard detection. His methods combine controlled laboratory vigilance paradigms, driving simulation, psychophysiological monitoring, and growth-curve and other quantitative modeling of performance over time.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Eric T. Greenlee, Texas Tech University (Human Factors Psychology Program, Department of Psychological Sciences), PhD University of Alabama 2015. Confirmed via Google Scholar profile (user=n21LEHsAAAAJ; ~951 citations), ResearchGate profile, and consistency with the pre-verified Human Factors papers (frequent co-author with Patricia R. DeLucia). All cited DOIs verified to resolve via Crossref with matching titles, venues, and Greenlee in the author list: recent_1 (Hess, Greenlee), recent_2 (Greenlee, Hess, Simpson, Finomore), recent_3 (McGarry et al., Greenlee among authors), seminal_1 and seminal_2 (Greenlee, DeLucia, Newton). Citation counts from Google Scholar / Human Factors metadata, retrieved 2026-06-17 (2018 hazard-detection paper ~143; 2018 effects-of-demands paper ~39). Note: an initially guessed DOI for 'Effects of task demands on tactile vigilance' (Experimental Brain Research 2023) did not resolve to that title, so that paper was excluded in favor of three independently verified recent papers. No unverifiable citations were included.
